sources.name: Wikipedia — Strider (1989 video game)
sources.notes: Genesis port of the 1989 Capcom arcade game. Mechanics confirmation: wall cling, slide, Cypher saber.

notes: Strider is a port of Capcom's 1989 arcade game. The Genesis version is considered one of the best home versions.
DEFINING MECHANIC: Hiryu is an acrobatic agent — wall cling, slide, and powerful plasma saber.
Hiryu's movement is among the most fluid and acrobatic of the 16-bit era.
The Cypher saber swings in a wide arc — hits enemies both in front of and above/below Hiryu depending on jump state.
No projectile weapon by default — all combat is melee saber range.
Walk and jump values not precisely documented from ROM — estimated from gameplay.

walk_speed.notes: Hiryu is fast — among the faster 16-bit protagonists. VERIFY from ROM.

slide.description: Down + attack. Hiryu slides forward at high speed, damaging enemies hit during the slide.
notes: Slide is a primary traversal and combat tool — simultaneously fast movement and hitbox-lowering attack.
The slide hitbox is very low — passes under most projectiles.
Key speedrun technique — slide-cancel allows maintaining slide momentum.

jump.notes: Hiryu has generous air control. Jump + saber attack creates aerial combat capability.

wall_cling._note: Core traversal mechanic. Hiryu can cling to walls and jump off them.
wall_cling.description: Press Hiryu against a wall while jumping — he clings to it. Press jump to launch off the wall.

types.ceiling_cling: Hiryu can also cling to ceilings in some sections (VERIFY)
types.wall_jump: Launch from wall in opposite direction

notes: Wall cling allows Hiryu to traverse vertical walls and reach elevated platforms.
Combined with the saber, Hiryu can attack from a wall-cling position.
Wall cling → jump creates the same alternating-wall traversal as Ninja Gaiden's wall jump.

cypher_saber._note: Hiryu's primary and only standard weapon. Plasma saber that swings in an arc.
cypher_saber.description: Wide-arc plasma blade. Swings in a large arc covering front and above/below Hiryu.
cypher_saber.attack_arc: Wide semi-circle in front of Hiryu — hits enemies at multiple heights
notes: No long-range projectiles by default — forces close engagement with all enemies.
The wide arc compensates for the lack of range — multiple enemies can be hit per swing.
Aerial saber swings during jumps change the arc orientation.
Slide + saber combo hits the low zone efficiently.

upgrades.cypher_b: Upgraded width or damage (VERIFY specific upgrade details)
upgrades.cypher_c: Final upgrade — larger arc, more damage (VERIFY)

robot_companions._note: Hiryu collects robotic animals during the game. They follow and assist in combat.
robot_companions.description: Small robot animals join Hiryu and attack nearby enemies automatically.
examples: Robot monkey — climbs to elevated positions and attacks
notes: Companions are passive — they follow and attack autonomously.
Multiple companions can be active simultaneously.
Companions die if hit by enemy attacks — they're temporary allies.
Non-linear stage order — after the first stage, can approach remaining stages in different orders (VERIFY).
Each stage has diverse vertical and horizontal traversal challenges requiring wall cling and saber.
Boss fights require pattern recognition for close-range saber combat.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Strider's acrobatic movement system (slide, wall cling, full air control, wide-arc saber) creates a high skill ceiling where mastery looks and feels dramatically different from basic play. A new player shuffles around enemies. A skilled player glides, slides, and slashes with almost no wasted motion.

The wide-arc Cypher saber compensates for the lack of ranged attacks by making melee safer — the arc is wide enough that Hiryu doesn't need to be precisely positioned. This is an elegant solution to the 'close-range only' constraint: if melee has enough range and arc, it approximates 'short-range ranged.'

The robot companions shift the tone of Strider from 'lone ninja' to 'ninja with loyal team.' Even if mechanically they're just floating damage-dealers, the animals' presence creates an emotional dynamic: protecting them becomes an unspoken goal even when it's not required.

Strider's cold war geopolitical aesthetic (Kazakhstan, Siberia, Antarctica) gives the game a distinct visual identity separate from fantasy or sci-fi. The world is recognizable but heightened — real locations, impossible threats. This is a design choice about how to frame a high-action game's setting.

The slide-as-both-traversal-and-attack is the kind of dual-purpose mechanic that makes a move set feel lean and purposeful. Every input serves multiple goals. Slide to cross a room fast. Slide to dodge a projectile. Slide to clear a low enemy. One button, three uses.
